seo.title: Guardrails vs Fine-Tuning for Agent Reliability
seo.description: Compare Runtime Guardrails and Fine-Tuning for AI agent reliability. Understand trade-offs and when to use each approach.

content.intro: When building reliable AI agents, teams often face a fundamental choice: shape model behavior before deployment through fine-tuning, or enforce constraints dynamically at runtime. Both approaches have legitimate use cases, and the right answer often depends on your specific reliability requirements, update cadence, and operational context.

approachA.name: Runtime Guardrails
approachA.description: Runtime guardrails intercept, inspect, and validate agent inputs and outputs as they occur, enforcing policies without modifying the underlying model. This approach treats the model as a black box and wraps it in a verification layer that can be updated independently. Vex is built around this philosophy, enabling teams to define and enforce behavioral contracts at execution time.
strengths: Policies can be updated instantly without retraining or redeployment of the model
Works across any model provider or version, avoiding vendor lock-in
Provides auditable logs of every enforcement decision for compliance and debugging
Catches emergent failure modes that weren't anticipated during training
weaknesses: Adds latency to every inference call due to interception and validation overhead
Cannot change the model's internal reasoning or knowledge, only its observable outputs
Complex rule sets can become difficult to maintain and may produce false positives
Relies on accurate detection logic; a poorly written guardrail can miss violations or over-block

approachB.name: Fine-Tuning
approachB.description: Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained model's weights using curated examples that demonstrate desired behaviors, effectively baking reliability constraints into the model itself. This can produce a model that naturally avoids certain failure modes without needing external enforcement. It is particularly effective when the target behavior is well-defined and training data is abundant.
strengths: Desired behaviors become intrinsic to the model, reducing reliance on external checks
Can improve response quality and tone consistency across a specific domain
No runtime overhead once the model is deployed, keeping latency low
Effective for encoding domain-specific knowledge that general models lack
weaknesses: Requires significant labeled data, compute resources, and ML expertise to execute well
Policy changes require a full retraining cycle, slowing iteration speed considerably
Fine-tuned models can still hallucinate or violate constraints in edge cases
Difficult to audit exactly why the model behaves a certain way after training

comparison.approachA: Policy changes deploy in minutes without touching the model
comparison.approachB: Requires a full retraining and redeployment cycle, often days or weeks

comparison.approachA: Every guardrail decision is logged with context and reasoning
comparison.approachB: Model internals are opaque; hard to trace why a specific output was produced

comparison.approachA: Adds measurable overhead per inference call
comparison.approachB: No runtime overhead once the model is deployed

comparison.approachA: Can detect and block novel failure patterns with updated rules
comparison.approachB: Only covers failure modes represented in training data

comparison.approachA: Low to moderate; requires defining policies and integrating the verification layer
comparison.approachB: High; demands curated datasets, compute budget, and ML engineering time

recommendation.heading: Which Should You Choose?
recommendation.summary: Fine-tuning is a strong investment when you have a stable, well-defined domain and the resources to maintain a training pipeline. Runtime guardrails are the better default for teams that need to move fast, operate across multiple models, or must demonstrate compliance through verifiable enforcement logs. In practice, the most robust production systems combine both: fine-tuning for baseline quality and runtime verification as a safety net for the unexpected.

cta.heading: Try Runtime Verification with Vex
cta.description: See how Vex's runtime guardrails can give your AI agents a verifiable safety layer in minutes, not months.
